You are a CLEAN-ROOM implementer. Your work will be legally relied upon as an
independent creation, and your complete tool-call transcript is retained as evidence. STRICT RULES:
1. Work ONLY inside your cluster directory. First action: read PROTOCOL.md at
   /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md, then your SPEC.md and interface/ files.
2. NEVER read any file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ (source, git, anything). The ONLY
   exceptions: EXECUTING the oracle scripts in your oracle/ dir, and READING files under your
   oracle/captured_responses/ or oracle/captures/ or oracle/examples/ (captured program output
   and observation inputs). Do not use git anywhere. Do not fetch tamarin-prover source or
   internals documentation from the web.
3. Do NOT reproduce tamarin-prover implementation internals from your training memory. Every
   behavior claim must trace to an oracle observation, a capture, or the SPEC. Exact output
   strings must be copied from OBSERVED oracle output (compatibility content). If you notice
   yourself recalling how tamarin-prover's source does something, discard that and observe instead.
4. Log every oracle interaction's purpose in workspace/QUERIES.log. Maintain workspace/BEHAVIOR.md
   as you learn. Design your own internal structure — do not try to guess the original's.
Your final structured report summarizes components, tests, coverage, and gaps.

Your cluster directory: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/weblayer/
CONTINUATION: a prior clean-room session already produced workspace/BEHAVIOR.md, workspace/QUERIES.log,
and the web-clean crate (12 src modules + tests/parity.rs + fixtures) but ended before finishing.
Read what exists in workspace/ FIRST (it is clean-room material you may build on), then continue per
SPEC.md: finish the implementation, make cargo test pass with real parity assertions against captures
(the current test run reports 0 tests executed — wire the fixtures into asserted tests), extend
BEHAVIOR.md coverage (route map, envelopes, main theory-view + proof-tree HTML), and keep logging
oracle queries. NOTE: hs_server.sh has a --port parsing bug; if you use the live server, invoke the
underlying binary with --port=<n> syntax via your own wrapper (black-box execution only, log it).
FINAL REPORT: write workspace/REPORT.md (components done, LOC, tests written/passing, oracle query
count, gaps, notes) and return as your final text a 10-line plain summary of it.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". - verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

No prover source was read. 6 7 Terms: **web layer** = the code that dispatches routes, escapes, and wraps 8 strings into envelopes/pages. **prover fragment** = a string the prover produced 9 (pretty-printed terms, constraint systems, method names, DOT bodies) that the 10 web layer embeds opaquely. This spec pins down the web layer; prover fragments 11 are treated as inputs. 12 13 --- 14 15 ## 1. Route grammar 16 17 Every dynamic route has the shape: 18 19 ``` 20 /thy//// 21 ``` 22 23 * `` — analysis kind. Only `trace` observed (a `diff` kind is 24 plausible but never appears in the corpus). 25 * `` — theory **version** selector. In real requests it is a decimal 26 number. The manifests store it as the literal token `#`; live probing shows 27 `%23` returns **404**, so `#` is the crawl tool's placeholder meaning "the 28 server's current version", and every URL the server *emits* uses the resolved 29 numeric index. Applying a proof method creates a new version, so the index 30 seen in a response's links is generally larger than the one requested. 31 * `` — selects the response family: 32 33 | handler | body kind | notes | 34 |-----------------------------|-----------|-------| 35 | `main/…` | JSON | AJAX content pane; 1	//! The `intdot/*` mini-page and the `interactive-graph-def/*` DOT skeleton.
2	//!
3	//! `intdot/<path>` returns a tiny standalone HTML page whose only content is a
4	//! `<dot-graph-viz>` custom element pointing at the matching
5	//! `interactive-graph-def/<path>` DOT source (same trailing path, resolved
6	//! numeric index). No trailing newline (ends `</html>`).
7	//!
8	//! `interactive-graph-def/<path>` returns Graphviz DOT. For a proof node with
9	//! no constructed graph (e.g. a freshly opened lemma) the body is the fixed
10	//! empty-graph skeleton below, which DOES end in a newline. Non-empty graphs
11	//! are prover-produced and out of scope for this template.
12	
13	const INTDOT_TEMPLATE: &str = r#"<!DOCTYPE html>
14	<html><head><meta charset="UTF-8" /><meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" /><title>Theory: §NAME§</title><style> body,html{width: 100%; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; margin: 0; padding: 0; }</style><link rel="stylesheet" href="/static/css/intdot-style.css"><script type="module" src="/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js"></script></script></head><body><dot-graph-viz dotsrc="§DOTSRC§"></dot-graph-viz>
15	</body></html>"#;
16	
17	/// The empty-graph DOT skeleton returned for a proof node with no graph.
18	pub const EMPTY_GRAPH_DOT: &str = "digraph \"G\" {\nnodesep=\"0.3\";\nranksep=\"0.3\";\nnode[fontsize=\"8\",fontname=\"Helvetica\",width=\"0.3\",height=\"0.2\"];\nedge[fontsize=\"8\",fontname=\"Helvetica\"];\n\n}\n";
19

21	///
22	/// * `theory_name` — used only in the `<title>` (not entity-escaped here since
23	///   the observed value is a plain identifier; escape upstream if needed).
24	/// * `dotsrc` — full path to the DOT source, e.g.
25	///   `/thy/trace/3/interactive-graph-def/proof/exec`.
26	pub fn render_intdot(theory_name: &str, dotsrc: &str) -> String {
27	    INTDOT_TEMPLATE
28	        .replace("§NAME§", theory_name)
29	        .replace("§DOTSRC§", dotsrc)
30	}

1	//! The full-page theory-view HTML shell returned by the `overview/*` routes.
2	//!
3	//! Observed structure (constant across theories after substituting the four
4	//! parameters below): a fixed `<head>` of stylesheet/script links, a north
5	//! header bar, then four layout panes — west "Proof scripts", east "Debug
6	//! information" (always empty), and center "Visualization display". The west
7	//! pane embeds the proof-script markup (see [`crate::proofscript`]); the center
8	//! pane embeds the currently-selected main content HTML (the same HTML the
9	//! corresponding `main/*` route returns in its JSON `html` field).
10	//!
11	//! Scaffolding constants in `shell_template` are byte-exact copies of oracle
12	//! output with four `§`-delimited slots: NAME, IDX, VERSION, FILENAME. The
13	//! body has no trailing newline (ends `</html>`). All internal links use the
14	//! resolved numeric theory index, never `#`.
15	
16	use crate::escape::html_escape;
17	use crate::shell_template::{PAGE_MID, PAGE_PREFIX, PAGE_TAIL};
18	
19	/// Parameters that vary between rendered pages.
20	pub struct PageParams<'a> {
21	    /// Theory name, shown in `<title>Theory: NAME</title>`.
22	    pub theory_name: &'a str,
23	    /// Resolved numeric theory index used in every internal URL.
24	    pub index: u64,
25	    /// Tamarin version string shown in the header (e.g. `"1.13.0"`).
26	    pub version: &'a str,
27	    /// Source filename used in the download / append links (e.g. `"foo.spthy"`).
28	    pub filename: &'a str,
29	}
30	
31	/// Render the full theory-view page from the shell parameters plus the already
32	/// rendered west (proof-script) and center (main content) pane inner HTML.
33	pub fn render_page(p: &PageParams, west_inner: &str, center_inner: &str) -> String {
34	    let idx = p.index.to_string();
35	    let prefix = PAGE_PREFIX
36	        .replace("§NAME§", &html_escape(p.theory_name))
37	        .replace("§IDX§", &idx)
38	        .replace("§VERSION§", p.version)
39	        .replace("§FILENAME§", p.filename);
40	    let mut out = String::with_capacity(
41	        prefix.len() + west_inner.len() + PAGE_MID.len() + center_inner.len() + PAGE_TAIL.len(),
42	    );
43	    out.push_str(&prefix);
44	    out.push_str(west_inner);
45	    out.push_str(PAGE_MID);
46	    out.push_str(center_inner);
47	    out.push_str(PAGE_TAIL);
48	    out
49	}

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Clean-room live-probe wrapper (black-box execution of the sanctioned oracle
# binary only). Works around the hs_server.sh --port bug by using --port=<n>.
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
BIN="/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover"
case "$1" in
  start)
    d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/"
    nohup "$BIN" interactive "$d" --port="$3" >/tmp/wl_server_$3.log 2>&1 &
    echo $! > /tmp/wl_server_$3.pid
    sleep 5
    echo "up on $3 (pid $(cat /tmp/wl_server_$3.pid))"
    ;;
  stop)
    kill "$(cat /tmp/wl_server_$2.pid)" 2>/dev/null
    rm -f /tmp/wl_server_$2.pid
    echo stopped
    ;;
esac
